title: Printing Text
subtitle: Output text with echo

sections.content: The `echo` command prints text to the screen. It sounds simple, but it's a building block for much more powerful techniques coming next.
sections.analogy: Think of `echo` as talking to the terminal — whatever you say, it repeats back to you.
keyPoints: `echo` prints whatever text you give it
Useful for seeing variable values and writing to files
What you `echo` can be redirected into files (coming in the next lesson)

sections.instruction: `echo` is the terminal's way of printing text. Whatever you type after `echo`, it repeats back to you. Simple, but incredibly useful.
sections.prompt: Type `echo "Hello, world"` to print a message.

onSuccess.message: The terminal printed your message right back. `echo` is like the terminal's version of a print statement. Developers use it in scripts to display status messages, and you'll soon use it to write text into files.

sections.instruction: Here's something cool: `echo` can read environment variables. `$USER` is a built-in variable that holds your username. The terminal replaces `$USER` with the actual value before printing.
sections.prompt: Type `echo "My name is $USER"` to see variable expansion in action.

onSuccess.message: The terminal replaced `$USER` with your actual username. Variables starting with `$` get expanded — the shell swaps them out before the command runs. This is how scripts adapt to different users and environments.

sections.instruction: You don't always need quotes. Without them, `echo` just prints whatever words follow it. Quotes matter when your text has special characters, but for plain words they're optional.
sections.prompt: Type `echo Hello world` without any quotes.

onSuccess.message: Same result, no quotes needed. The shell treats `Hello` and `world` as separate arguments, but `echo` just prints them all with spaces between. Quotes become important when you have special characters like `$`, `*`, or want to preserve extra spaces.

sections.prompt: To print your username using the echo command, you type: echo ___

completionMessage: You now know `echo` — the terminal's print command. It seems simple, but it's a building block for everything: writing to files, debugging scripts, and checking variables. Next up, you'll learn to search through files.
